summary: Gruber critiques Microsoft's process of selecting Aptos (formerly Bierstadt) as the new default font for Office, replacing the 15-year-old Calibri. He argues that companies with taste don't conduct design decisions via public surveys, and suspects Microsoft knew their choice all along. He created his own specimen PDFs to properly show the fonts at text sizes, since Microsoft had only displayed them at large display sizes. Of the five candidates, he considers Seaford the superior choice and finds no compelling reason Calibri needed replacing at all. The piece includes detailed typographic analysis of each candidate font, with pointed comparisons to industry-standard typefaces.
key_points: Microsoft's default font choices affect everyone who reads documents, not just Office users, making this a consequential design decision
Companies with genuine taste don't conduct design by survey — Gruber suspects Microsoft predetermined the Aptos choice and the public feedback was theater
Microsoft oddly never properly showed the new fonts at text sizes, which is where they actually matter for Office documents
Gruber argues Calibri was perfectly fine and didn't need replacing — a good default font should last decades, as Apple's Helvetica/Helvetica Neue has since 1991
Of the five candidates, Gruber considers Seaford the only other credible choice and the one Microsoft would have picked if they had better taste
money_quotes: I have never regularly used any Office app other than Excel, and that was over 20 years ago. But it's impossible not to encounter documents created with Office, whether you personally use it or not. Thus, Microsoft's typographic choices affect us all.
Companies that have taste do not conduct design via surveys.
My guess is they're full of shit and probably knew all along they were going to go with Aptos/Bierstadt, the obvious choice, from the start.

I don't know why Microsoft states as fact that Calibri somehow needed to be replaced as their default font just because it's 15 years old. A good default font should stand the test of time for decades, if not a literal lifetime.
If Microsoft had better (any?) taste, they would have chosen Seaford.
Looks like it came off a clip art CD circa 1995.
Too friendly, bordering on childish, to serve as the default for Office.
No one is going to make a movie about Aptos.
key_insight: Microsoft's font selection process — replacing a perfectly good default via public survey rather than exercising genuine design taste — reveals a fundamental difference between companies that have aesthetic convictions and those that outsource them.
